concepts: vertical: bigger machine
horizontal: more machines
horizontal scaling requires statelessness
cost and complexity tradeoffs
questionPatterns: Why is horizontal scaling generally preferred for web servers over vertical scaling?
What does an application need architecturally to scale horizontally?
evaluationCriteria: correctly explains both scaling approaches
identifies statelessness as a prerequisite for horizontal scaling
understands vertical scaling has a hard ceiling

concepts: Layer 4 (transport) vs Layer 7 (application) load balancing
round robin, least connections, IP hash algorithms
health checks removing unhealthy instances
questionPatterns: What's the difference between a Layer 4 and Layer 7 load balancer?
How does a load balancer know to stop routing traffic to a crashed server?
evaluationCriteria: correctly distinguishes L4 (routes on IP/port) from L7 (routes on content, e.g. URL path)
names at least one balancing algorithm correctly
explains health check mechanism

concepts: CDN caches static assets at edge locations
Redis caches computed/DB data server-side
browser cache reduces repeat requests entirely
cache-control headers
questionPatterns: Where would you introduce caching in a typical web app's request path, from browser to database?
What's the purpose of a CDN versus an in-memory cache like Redis?
evaluationCriteria: identifies at least 2-3 caching layers correctly (browser, CDN, application/Redis)
explains CDN's role in serving static assets close to users
distinguishes CDN (static content) from Redis (dynamic/computed data)

concepts: splitting data across multiple database servers
shard key selection
hotspot risk with poor shard key choice
cross-shard query complexity
questionPatterns: How would you shard a users collection that's grown too large for one server?
What makes a good shard key, and what happens if you pick a bad one?
evaluationCriteria: explains sharding as horizontal data partitioning
identifies shard key needs even distribution to avoid hotspots
acknowledges cross-shard queries/joins become harder

concepts: decoupling producers from consumers
async processing of background work
at-least-once vs exactly-once delivery
use case: sending notification emails without blocking the request
questionPatterns: Why would you put email sending on a message queue instead of doing it directly in the request handler?
What's the difference between at-least-once and exactly-once delivery, and why does it matter?
evaluationCriteria: explains decoupling and non-blocking benefit correctly
gives a valid async-processing use case
shows awareness that most queues guarantee at-least-once, requiring idempotent consumers

concepts: independent deployability vs simpler operations
network overhead and distributed system complexity in microservices
when a monolith is the right choice for a small team
your own project split (Node backend + separate Python AI service)
questionPatterns: Your project has a separate Python AI service alongside the Node backend — is that microservices, and why did you split it that way?
What complexity does splitting a monolith into microservices introduce?
evaluationCriteria: gives an honest, grounded answer about their own architecture rather than overclaiming 'microservices'
names real distributed-system costs (network latency, partial failure, deployment complexity)
justifies the split with a real reason (language/library needs, e.g. Python AI ecosystem)

concepts: single entry point for multiple backend services
cross-cutting concerns: auth, rate limiting, logging
routing requests to the right service
questionPatterns: What problems does an API Gateway solve in a multi-service architecture?
If you had five microservices, would clients call each directly or through a gateway, and why?
evaluationCriteria: explains centralizing cross-cutting concerns as the main benefit
understands it simplifies the client's view of a multi-service backend
can reason about this even without direct prior implementation

concepts: token bucket allows bursts up to a cap
sliding window is more precise but costlier to compute
fixed window is simplest but allows boundary bursts
questionPatterns: What's the difference between a fixed window and sliding window rate limiter?
Why might a fixed window rate limiter let through double the intended requests at a window boundary?
evaluationCriteria: correctly explains the boundary-burst flaw of fixed window
distinguishes at least two algorithms correctly
can reason about the precision-vs-cost tradeoff between them

concepts: minimizes remapping when nodes are added/removed
hash ring concept
use case: distributed caching, sharding
questionPatterns: Why is consistent hashing preferred over simple modulo hashing when scaling a distributed cache?
What happens to key distribution when you add a node with consistent hashing versus modulo hashing?
evaluationCriteria: explains modulo hashing requires remapping almost everything when node count changes
describes the hash ring concept at a basic level
understands the benefit: only a fraction of keys remap on node changes

concepts: base62 encoding vs hashing for short codes
collision handling
read-heavy system, so caching/replication matters more than write scaling
redirect status code choice (301 vs 302)
questionPatterns: Walk me through how you'd design a URL shortener like bit.ly, focusing on how short codes are generated.
Is a URL shortener read-heavy or write-heavy, and how does that shape your design?
evaluationCriteria: proposes a reasonable short-code generation strategy (counter+base62, or hash+collision check)
correctly identifies the system as read-heavy
connects that to caching/replication being prioritized over write throughput

concepts: where to enforce it: gateway vs per-service
storing counters in Redis for distributed consistency
algorithm choice tradeoff (token bucket vs sliding window)
questionPatterns: How would you design a rate limiter that works correctly across multiple server instances?
Why can't you just keep a rate limit counter in each server's local memory?
evaluationCriteria: identifies the need for a shared store (Redis) across instances
explains local in-memory counters break under horizontal scaling
picks and justifies an algorithm

topic: CAP Theorem in System Design Context
concepts: applying CAP tradeoffs to real design decisions
choosing AP vs CP based on product requirements
example: chat delivery (AP) vs payment processing (CP)
questionPatterns: Would you design a chat app's message delivery to favor consistency or availability, and why?
Give an example of a feature where you'd deliberately choose availability over strict consistency.
evaluationCriteria: applies CAP correctly to a concrete product scenario, not just the abstract theorem
justifies the choice with real user-experience reasoning
shows they understand this is a deliberate design decision, not a default

concepts: edge servers close to users reduce latency
caching static assets
origin server fallback on cache miss
questionPatterns: How does a CDN reduce load time for users far from your origin server?
What happens on a CDN cache miss?
evaluationCriteria: explains edge-location proximity reducing latency correctly
understands cache miss falls back to origin and then populates the edge cache
gives a reasonable example of CDN-appropriate content (images, JS bundles)

concepts: fan-out on write vs fan-out on read
queue-based async delivery
multiple channels (email, push, in-app)
your own wishlist/overdue-book notification cron experience
questionPatterns: You built notification systems for book requests and wishlists — how would that design change at a much larger scale, say millions of users?
What's the difference between fan-out on write and fan-out on read for delivering notifications?
evaluationCriteria: connects the answer back to their real cron-based notification experience as a starting point
explains fan-out tradeoff (write-heavy vs read-heavy) correctly
proposes queue-based decoupling for scale

concepts: persistent WebSocket connections per user
message delivery guarantees and ordering
horizontal scaling challenge: routing a message to the right server holding that user's connection
your own Socket.io experience as a starting point
questionPatterns: You've used Socket.io for real-time notifications — how would you scale that to millions of concurrent connections across multiple servers?
What problem arises when you horizontally scale a WebSocket server, and how would you solve it?
evaluationCriteria: grounds the answer in their real Socket.io experience
identifies the cross-server routing problem (a message needs to reach a specific server holding a specific connection)
proposes a plausible solution (pub/sub layer like Redis, sticky sessions)
